1Ôćĺ# Manual Section Integrity
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Principle
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺSections marked with `[MANUAL]` delimiters contain developer-authored content that the forge never modifies, regenerates, or removes. The merge algorithm treats \[MANUAL\] blocks as inviolable during updates ÔÇö they are preserved in place, and any conflict between generated content and manual content is escalated to the user.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Rationale
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺGenerated skills cover what can be extracted from source code, but developers often need to add context that no tool can infer: architectural rationale, migration guides, team conventions, known gotchas. The \[MANUAL\] marker system gives developers a safe zone for hand-written content that survives regeneration cycles.
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺWithout \[MANUAL\] preservation:
    12Ôćĺ- Developer-authored content is overwritten on every update-skill run
    13Ôćĺ- Users stop adding valuable context because it disappears
    14Ôćĺ- Skills become purely mechanical extractions with no human insight
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺWith \[MANUAL\] preservation:
    17Ôćĺ- Hand-written content persists across unlimited regeneration cycles
    18Ôćĺ- Developers invest in skill quality knowing their work is protected
    19Ôćĺ- Skills combine machine-extracted precision with human-authored context
    20Ôćĺ
    21Ôćĺ## Marker Format
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ\[MANUAL\] sections use HTML comment delimiters:
    24Ôćĺ
    25Ôćĺ```markdown
    26Ôćĺ<!-- [MANUAL:section-name] -->
    27Ôćĺ
    28ÔćĺDeveloper-authored content here. This content is never modified
    29Ôćĺby the forge during updates or regeneration.
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ<!-- [/MANUAL:section-name] -->
    32Ôćĺ```
    33Ôćĺ
    34Ôćĺ**Rules:**
    35Ôćĺ- `section-name` must be unique within the file
    36Ôćĺ- Opening and closing markers must match exactly
    37Ôćĺ- Multiple \[MANUAL\] blocks allowed per file
    38Ôćĺ- Nested \[MANUAL\] blocks are forbidden ÔÇö trigger an ERROR if detected
    39Ôćĺ- Markers apply to any output file: `SKILL.md`, `references/*.md`, integration files
    40Ôćĺ
    41Ôćĺ### [MANUAL] Subdirectories
    42Ôćĺ
    43ÔćĺUser-authored scripts and assets are placed in `scripts/[MANUAL]/` and `assets/[MANUAL]/` subdirectories within the skill package. These follow the same preservation principle as markdown `<!-- [MANUAL] -->` markers:
    44Ôćĺ
    45Ôćĺ- Files in `[MANUAL]/` subdirectories are preserved unconditionally during `update-skill`
    46Ôćĺ- Source-derived scripts/assets (outside `[MANUAL]/`) are refreshed from source during updates
    47Ôćĺ- Conflicts (user file has same name as source file outside `[MANUAL]/`) are flagged as HIGH severity
    48Ôćĺ
    49Ôćĺ## Pattern Examples
    50Ôćĺ
    51Ôćĺ### Example 1: Adding Manual Context to a Generated Skill
    52Ôćĺ
    53Ôćĺ**Context:** A developer wants to add migration notes to an API function that the forge generated.
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ**Implementation:**
    56Ôćĺ```markdown
    57Ôćĺ## `migrateDatabase(config: MigrateConfig): Promise<void>`
    58Ôćĺ
    59ÔćĺRuns pending database migrations in sequence.
    60Ôćĺ
    61Ôćĺ**Parameters:**
    62Ôćĺ| Name | Type | Required | Default |
    63Ôćĺ| --- | --- | --- | --- |
    64Ôćĺ| config | `MigrateConfig` | yes | ÔÇö |
    65Ôćĺ
    66Ôćĺ[AST:src/migrate.ts:L15]
    67Ôćĺ
    68Ôćĺ<!-- [MANUAL:migrate-notes] -->
    69Ôćĺ
    70Ôćĺ### Migration Gotchas
    71Ôćĺ
    72Ôćĺ- Always run `backupDatabase()` before migration in production
    73Ôćĺ- Migrations are not reversible after v2.3.0 ÔÇö the rollback API was removed
    74Ôćĺ- If using connection pooling, set `pool.max = 1` during migration to avoid lock conflicts
    75Ôćĺ
    76Ôćĺ<!-- [/MANUAL:migrate-notes] -->
    77Ôćĺ```
    78Ôćĺ
    79Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
    80Ôćĺ- The \[MANUAL\] block sits naturally within the generated skill structure
    81Ôćĺ- Content inside the markers is entirely developer-controlled
    82Ôćĺ- The forge regenerates everything outside the markers; the block is re-inserted in place
    83Ôćĺ
    84Ôćĺ### Example 2: Merge Algorithm During Update
    85Ôćĺ
    86Ôćĺ**Context:** The update-skill workflow needs to regenerate a skill while preserving \[MANUAL\] blocks.
    87Ôćĺ
    88Ôćĺ**Implementation:** The merge follows this sequence:
    89Ôćĺ1. **Extract** all \[MANUAL\] blocks from the existing skill, recording their section-name and parent section context
    90Ôćĺ2. **Map** each block to its parent section in the document structure
    91Ôćĺ3. **Regenerate** the skill content from fresh source extraction
    92Ôćĺ4. **Re-insert** \[MANUAL\] blocks into their original positions relative to parent sections
    93Ôćĺ5. **Flag conflicts** if any arise (see conflict types below)
    94Ôćĺ
    95Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
    96Ôćĺ- \[MANUAL\] blocks are extracted before regeneration and re-inserted after
    97Ôćĺ- Position is determined by parent section, not absolute line number
    98Ôćĺ- The merge is deterministic ÔÇö same inputs always produce same output
    99Ôćĺ
   100Ôćĺ### Example 3: Conflict Resolution
   101Ôćĺ
   102Ôćĺ**Context:** Regeneration produces content that conflicts with existing \[MANUAL\] blocks.
   103Ôćĺ
   104Ôćĺ**Implementation:** Four conflict severity levels:
   105Ôćĺ
   106Ôćĺ| Severity | Condition | Resolution |
   107Ôćĺ| --- | --- | --- |
   108Ôćĺ| HIGH | Regenerated content overlaps \[MANUAL\] position | Present both versions, user chooses |
   109Ôćĺ| HIGH | Parent section deleted, \[MANUAL\] block orphaned | Flag orphaned block, user decides to keep or remove |
   110Ôćĺ| MEDIUM | \[MANUAL\] references a deleted export | Flag stale reference for user review |
   111Ôćĺ| LOW | New export generated adjacent to \[MANUAL\] block | Auto-resolve: place new content before the block |
   112Ôćĺ
   113Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
   114Ôćĺ- HIGH conflicts always require user input ÔÇö never auto-resolved
   115Ôćĺ- MEDIUM conflicts are flagged but do not block the update
   116Ôćĺ- LOW conflicts are auto-resolved with a deterministic rule (new content before block)
   117Ôćĺ
   118Ôćĺ### Example 4: Orphan Detection
   119Ôćĺ
   120Ôćĺ**Context:** An export that contained a \[MANUAL\] block has been removed from source.
   121Ôćĺ
   122Ôćĺ**Implementation:**
   123Ôćĺ```
   124ÔćĺWARNING: Orphaned [MANUAL] block detected
   125Ôćĺ
   126Ôćĺ  Section: [MANUAL:migrate-notes]
   127Ôćĺ  Previous parent: ## `migrateDatabase(config: MigrateConfig): Promise<void>`
   128Ôćĺ  Status: Parent export removed from source
   129Ôćĺ
   130Ôćĺ  Options:
   131Ôćĺ  1. Keep block in a "Legacy Notes" section at end of skill
   132Ôćĺ  2. Remove block (content will be shown for copy before deletion)
   133Ôćĺ  3. Relocate to a different section (specify target)
   134Ôćĺ```
   135Ôćĺ
   136Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
   137Ôćĺ- Orphaned blocks are never silently deleted
   138Ôćĺ- The user always sees the content and chooses its fate
   139Ôćĺ- Option 1 preserves the content without cluttering the active skill sections
   140Ôćĺ
   141Ôćĺ## Audit Interaction
   142Ôćĺ
   143ÔćĺThe audit-skill workflow detects \[MANUAL\] section integrity issues:
   144Ôćĺ- **Malformed markers**: Missing closing tag, mismatched names
   145Ôćĺ- **Nested markers**: \[MANUAL\] inside \[MANUAL\] ÔÇö always an error
   146Ôćĺ- **Stale references**: \[MANUAL\] content mentioning exports that no longer exist
   147Ôćĺ- **Orphaned blocks**: Parent section no longer in the skill
   148Ôćĺ
   149ÔćĺThese findings appear in the drift report with appropriate severity classifications.
   150Ôćĺ
   151Ôćĺ## Anti-Patterns
   152Ôćĺ
   153Ôćĺ- Modifying content inside \[MANUAL\] markers during automated updates ÔÇö always preserve exactly
   154Ôćĺ- Silently removing orphaned \[MANUAL\] blocks ÔÇö always escalate to user
   155Ôćĺ- Nesting \[MANUAL\] blocks ÔÇö one level only, nesting is an error
   156Ôćĺ- Using \[MANUAL\] markers in provenance-map.json or metadata.json ÔÇö markers are for markdown content files only
   157Ôćĺ
   158Ôćĺ## Related Fragments
   159Ôćĺ
   160Ôćĺ- [provenance-tracking.md](provenance-tracking.md) ÔÇö how \[MANUAL\] sections interact with provenance (no source location, author: "manual")
   161Ôćĺ- [zero-hallucination.md](zero-hallucination.md) ÔÇö \[MANUAL\] sections are the designated space for human-authored claims
   162Ôćĺ- [skill-lifecycle.md](skill-lifecycle.md) ÔÇö \[MANUAL\] preservation is critical in the update and audit phases
   163Ôćĺ
   164Ôćĺ_Source: consolidated from manual-section-rules.md, merge-conflict-rules.md, and update-skill steps_
   165Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# CCC Bridge
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Principle
     4Ôćĺ
     5Ôćĺ`ccc_bridge.*` references in workflow steps are **conceptual interfaces**, not callable functions. They describe a semantic code discovery operation to perform. Use the `ccc` MCP server tools (when available) or `ccc` CLI commands to execute these operations. See the TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK rule ÔÇö if ccc is unavailable, the calling step falls back to direct ast-grep or source reading without ccc pre-discovery.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Rationale
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺWithout ccc pre-discovery, extraction steps scan all source files uniformly ÔÇö processing them in directory order or entry-point-first order. On large codebases (500+ files), this means AST extraction in CLI streaming mode uses `head -N` cutoffs that may miss relevant exports in files that appear late in the scan. Integration detection in Stack Skill relies on grep-based co-import counting, which misses semantic relationships between libraries that don't appear in the same file.
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺWith ccc pre-discovery:
    12Ôćĺ- Extraction steps receive a relevance-ranked file queue ÔÇö the most semantically important files are processed first, before any streaming cutoff
    13Ôćĺ- Integration detection gains semantic augmentation ÔÇö pairs below the 2-file co-import threshold can be evaluated via natural language queries
    14Ôćĺ- Audit workflows can detect renamed/moved exports via semantic search before classifying them as deleted
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺThe key architectural constraint: ccc discovers, ast-grep verifies. Discovery method is orthogonal to confidence tier. This keeps the 4-tier confidence system (T1/T1-low/T2/T3) clean and avoids tier proliferation.
    17Ôćĺ
    18Ôćĺ## When ccc Is Used
    19Ôćĺ
    20Ôćĺccc is a **discovery layer only**. It answers "where should I look?" ÔÇö it does not produce citations or structural claims. Every path or symbol returned by ccc_bridge must be verified by `ast_bridge` (T1) or source reading (T1-low) before it enters the extraction inventory. ccc results never appear in provenance citations.
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺccc is **required** at the Forge+ tier (it defines Forge+) and **optionally available** at the Deep tier as an enhancement (when `tools.ccc: true` in forge-tier.yaml).
    23Ôćĺ
    24Ôćĺ## Availability
    25Ôćĺ
    26Ôćĺccc_bridge operations are available when:
    27Ôćĺ- `tools.ccc: true` in forge-tier.yaml (verified by `ccc --help` + `ccc doctor` in setup-forge)
    28Ôćĺ- `ccc_index.status` is `"fresh"` or `"stale"` in forge-tier.yaml (an index exists for the project)
    29Ôćĺ
    30ÔćĺWhen either condition is false, calling steps skip ccc discovery silently and proceed with direct ast-grep or source reading. This is standard Forge tier behavior ÔÇö not a degradation.
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ## Operations
    33Ôćĺ
    34Ôćĺ### `ccc_bridge.search(query, path?, top_k?)`
    35Ôćĺ
    36Ôćĺ**Resolves to:** `ccc search "{query}" --path {path} --top {top_k}` (CLI) or the `ccc` MCP search tool (preferred)
    37Ôćĺ
    38ÔćĺReturns: list of `{file, score, snippet}` entries ranked by semantic relevance to the query. These are **candidates** for ast-grep extraction ÔÇö not verified exports.
    39Ôćĺ
    40Ôćĺ**Usage context:** Called before ast-grep in Forge+ and Deep tier extraction steps to discover semantically relevant source regions. Results pre-rank the file extraction queue so ast-grep processes the most relevant files first.
    41Ôćĺ
    42Ôćĺ### `ccc_bridge.ensure_index(path)`
    43Ôćĺ
    44Ôćĺ**Resolves to:** Check `ccc_index.status` in forge-tier.yaml. If `"none"` or the indexed_path does not match, run `ccc init {path}` then `ccc index` and update forge-tier.yaml.
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ**Usage context:** Called by setup-forge step-01b to ensure the project root is indexed. Called lazily by extraction steps when `ccc_index.status` is `"none"` but ccc is available.
    47Ôćĺ
    48Ôćĺ### `ccc_bridge.status()`
    49Ôćĺ
    50Ôćĺ**Resolves to:** Two-step verification:
    51Ôćĺ1. `ccc --help` ÔÇö confirms binary exists (exit 0)
    52Ôćĺ2. `ccc doctor` ÔÇö confirms daemon is running, extracts version string, validates embedding model
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ**Usage context:** Called exclusively by setup-forge step-01 during tool detection. Downstream workflows read the result from forge-tier.yaml ÔÇö they do not re-verify.
    55Ôćĺ
    56Ôćĺ## Confidence
    57Ôćĺ
    58Ôćĺccc discovery does not produce a confidence tier. The provenance chain is:
    59Ôćĺ
    60Ôćĺ1. ccc discovers candidate files (internal hint ÔÇö not cited)
    61Ôćĺ2. ast-grep verifies exports in those files Ôćĺ **T1** citation `[AST:file:Lnn]`
    62Ôćĺ3. Or source reading verifies Ôćĺ **T1-low** citation `[SRC:file:Lnn]`
    63Ôćĺ
    64ÔćĺThe ccc search is invisible in the output artifact. A Forge+ skill's citations are indistinguishable from a Forge skill's citations ÔÇö the difference is in extraction coverage, not citation format.
    65Ôćĺ
    66Ôćĺ## Indexing Lifecycle
    67Ôćĺ
    68Ôćĺ### When Indexing Happens
    69Ôćĺ
    70Ôćĺ1. **setup-forge step-01b:** Indexes the project root when setup-forge runs. This is the primary indexing point.
    71Ôćĺ2. **Workflow discovery steps:** If `ccc_index.status` is `"stale"` or `"none"`, discovery steps trigger a re-index and warn the user. They do not block.
    72Ôćĺ3. **ccc daemon:** Incremental indexing means re-indexing unchanged files is a near-no-op.
    73Ôćĺ
    74Ôćĺ### Freshness
    75Ôćĺ
    76Ôćĺ- Staleness threshold: 24 hours (configurable via `ccc_index.staleness_threshold_hours` in forge-tier.yaml)
    77Ôćĺ- A stale index still produces useful results ÔÇö the workflow proceeds with the stale index and notes the staleness
    78Ôćĺ- setup-forge is the designated refresh authority
    79Ôćĺ
    80Ôćĺ### Relationship to QMD Registry
    81Ôćĺ
    82Ôćĺccc_index and qmd_collections are **orthogonal**:
    83Ôćĺ- `ccc_index` in forge-tier.yaml tracks the persistent source code index (one per project)
    84Ôćĺ- `qmd_collections[]` in forge-tier.yaml tracks per-skill workflow artifact collections
    85Ôćĺ- ccc indexes source code for semantic search; QMD indexes curated artifacts for temporal/knowledge search
    86Ôćĺ- The janitor role for QMD (setup-forge step-03) operates independently of ccc_index
    87Ôćĺ
    88Ôćĺ## Query Volume Bounds
    89Ôćĺ
    90ÔćĺTo prevent excessive daemon calls, workflow steps cap ccc queries:
    91Ôćĺ- **create-skill extraction:** max 2 queries per skill (discovery + optional scope refinement)
    92Ôćĺ- **analyze-source mapping:** max 1 query per qualifying unit
    93Ôćĺ- **create-stack-skill integration detection:** max 1 query per library pair
    94Ôćĺ- **audit-skill re-index:** max 1 query per export missing from its recorded location
    95Ôćĺ
    96Ôćĺ## Anti-Patterns
    97Ôćĺ
    98Ôćĺ- Using ccc_bridge results as citations without ast-grep verification ÔÇö ccc output is never a provenance citation
    99Ôćĺ- Blocking a workflow because ccc is unavailable ÔÇö ccc is always optional
   100Ôćĺ- Running ccc_bridge.ensure_index() without checking ccc_index.status first ÔÇö unnecessary re-indexing
   101Ôćĺ- Passing ccc results directly to the extraction inventory ÔÇö they are candidates, not extractions
   102Ôćĺ- Listing ccc as "unavailable" in reports for Quick/Forge tiers ÔÇö ccc is a Forge+ capability, not something Quick/Forge tiers are missing
   103Ôćĺ
   104Ôćĺ## Related Fragments
   105Ôćĺ
   106Ôćĺ- [progressive-capability.md](progressive-capability.md) ÔÇö Forge+ tier definition and positive framing
   107Ôćĺ- [confidence-tiers.md](confidence-tiers.md) ÔÇö why ccc does not create a new confidence tier
   108Ôćĺ- [qmd-registry.md](qmd-registry.md) ÔÇö the parallel but separate registry for QMD collections
   109Ôćĺ
   110Ôćĺ_Source: designed as part of the Forge+ tier integration for cocoindex-code semantic code search_
   111Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# SKF Knowledge Overview
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Principle
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺAgent-level knowledge files capture cross-cutting principles and patterns that Ferris applies across multiple workflows. They are distinct from workflow `data/` files, which contain step-specific operational artifacts. Knowledge is loaded just-in-time when a workflow step directs ÔÇö never preloaded into context.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Rationale
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺThe SKF module has 12 workflows containing numerous operational `data/` files embedded in step directories. These data files define schemas, templates, scoring formulas, and extraction rules tightly coupled to individual steps. But several principles ÔÇö zero hallucination, confidence tiers, provenance ÔÇö cut across multiple workflows and need a single authoritative source rather than fragmented repetition.
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺWithout a knowledge folder:
    12Ôćĺ- Cross-cutting principles get duplicated across workflow steps
    13Ôćĺ- Updates to foundational rules require changes in multiple locations
    14Ôćĺ- Ferris has no centralized reference for judgment calls that span workflows
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺWith knowledge files:
    17Ôćĺ- Each principle has one canonical definition
    18Ôćĺ- Workflow steps reference knowledge files instead of re-stating rules
    19Ôćĺ- Ferris loads only the knowledge relevant to the current task
    20Ôćĺ
    21Ôćĺ## Knowledge Map
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ| File                                                       | Purpose                                                           | Key Workflows                      |
    24Ôćĺ|------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
    25Ôćĺ| [zero-hallucination.md](zero-hallucination.md)             | Every claim traces to source; uncitable content excluded          | AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, RA |
    26Ôćĺ| [confidence-tiers.md](confidence-tiers.md)                 | T1/T1-low/T2/T3 trust model and citation formats                  | AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, EX, RA, VS |
    27Ôćĺ| [progressive-capability.md](progressive-capability.md)     | Quick/Forge/Forge+/Deep tier philosophy and behavior adaptation          | SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, EX, VS, RA |
    28Ôćĺ| [agentskills-spec.md](agentskills-spec.md)                 | agentskills.io output format principles and compliance            | CS, QS, SS, US, TS, EX             |
    29Ôćĺ| [skill-lifecycle.md](skill-lifecycle.md)                   | End-to-end pipeline, artifact flow, workflow selection            | All 12                             |
    30Ôćĺ| [provenance-tracking.md](provenance-tracking.md)           | Provenance-map.json entries and file_entries, evidence reports, claim traceability | CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS             |
    31Ôćĺ| [manual-section-integrity.md](manual-section-integrity.md) | \[MANUAL\] marker preservation and merge algorithm                | CS, US, AS, TS, EX                 |
    32Ôćĺ| [qmd-registry.md](qmd-registry.md)                         | Progressive QMD collection registry and collection gate principle | SF, BS, CS, US, AS                 |
    33Ôćĺ| [ccc-bridge.md](ccc-bridge.md)                             | ccc_bridge interface, indexing lifecycle, Forge+ discovery-before-extraction | SF, AN, CS, SS, US, AS             |
    34Ôćĺ| [architecture-verification.md](architecture-verification.md) | Pre-code verification pipeline, integration verdicts, iteration loop | VS, RA, SS                        |
    35Ôćĺ| [doc-fetcher.md](doc-fetcher.md)                           | T3 external documentation fetching, quarantine rules, tool-agnostic | CS, QS                             |
    36Ôćĺ| [split-body-strategy.md](split-body-strategy.md)           | Selective vs full split-body, detection pattern, agent accuracy   | CS, QS, US, TS, EX                |
    37Ôćĺ| [overview.md](overview.md)                                 | Knowledge map and JiT loading protocol (this file)               | All (entry point)                  |
    38Ôćĺ
    39Ôćĺ## JiT Loading Protocol
    40Ôćĺ
    41Ôćĺ1. Ferris receives a task or workflow activation
    42Ôćĺ2. Consult `skf-knowledge-index.csv` to identify relevant knowledge fragments by tags or description. The `tier` column classifies fragments as `core` (always relevant when the domain applies) or `extended` (load only when the specific scenario is active ÔÇö e.g., `doc-fetcher.md` only when `doc_urls` are present in the brief)
    43Ôćĺ3. Load only the files needed for the current task ÔÇö never bulk-load all knowledge
    44Ôćĺ4. Apply knowledge principles during workflow execution alongside step-specific `data/` files
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ## Knowledge vs. Data
    47Ôćĺ
    48Ôćĺ| Aspect    | Knowledge Files (`knowledge/`)       | Data Files (`data/`)                    |
    49Ôćĺ|-----------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
    50Ôćĺ| Scope     | Cross-cutting, multi-workflow        | Single workflow, single step            |
    51Ôćĺ| Content   | Principles, patterns, judgment rules | Schemas, templates, scoring formulas    |
    52Ôćĺ| Loading   | JiT via index lookup                 | JiT via step instruction                |
    53Ôćĺ| Examples  | Zero hallucination, confidence tiers | skill-brief-schema.md, scoring-rules.md |
    54Ôćĺ| Stability | Rarely changes                       | Changes with workflow evolution         |
    55Ôćĺ
    56Ôćĺ## Related Fragments
    57Ôćĺ
    58Ôćĺ- [confidence-tiers.md](confidence-tiers.md) ÔÇö the trust model that underpins extraction and scoring
    59Ôćĺ- [skill-lifecycle.md](skill-lifecycle.md) ÔÇö the end-to-end pipeline connecting all workflows
    60Ôćĺ- [progressive-capability.md](progressive-capability.md) ÔÇö tier philosophy that affects every workflow
    61Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# Progressive Capability
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Principle
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺEvery capability tier ÔÇö Quick, Forge, Forge+, and Deep ÔÇö produces legitimate, useful skills. Tiers describe what tools are available, not a quality hierarchy. A Quick skill is complete at its tier; a Deep skill adds depth, not correctness. Framing is always positive: describe what each tier enables, never what it lacks.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Rationale
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺSkill Forge operates in diverse environments. Some users have ast-grep, GitHub CLI, and QMD installed; others have none. The progressive capability model ensures Ferris adapts to the environment without making users feel their setup is insufficient.
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺWithout progressive capability:
    12Ôćĺ- Users without ast-grep see error messages or degraded output
    13Ôćĺ- Quick tier skills feel like failures rather than valid deliverables
    14Ôćĺ- Workflow steps contain conditional logic for missing tools scattered throughout
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺWith progressive capability:
    17Ôćĺ- Each tier has a clear identity and positive capability description
    18Ôćĺ- Workflow behavior adapts cleanly based on the detected tier
    19Ôćĺ- Users understand what their tier enables and what upgrading would add
    20Ôćĺ
    21Ôćĺ## Tier Definitions
    22Ôćĺ
    23Ôćĺ| Tier | Requirements | Capabilities | Identity |
    24Ôćĺ| --- | --- | --- | --- |
    25Ôćĺ| Quick | No tools required | Fast, template-driven, package-name resolution | Speed-first skill creation |
    26Ôćĺ| Forge | ast-grep available | AST-backed structural analysis, line-level citations | Precision-focused compilation |
    27Ôćĺ| Forge+ | ast-grep + ccc | Semantic discovery + AST verification, pre-ranked extraction | Intelligent precision compilation |
    28Ôćĺ| Deep | ast-grep + gh + qmd | AST + GitHub exploration + QMD synthesis, maximum provenance | Full intelligence pipeline |
    29Ôćĺ
    30Ôćĺ## Pattern Examples
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ### Example 1: Tool Detection and Tier Assignment
    33Ôćĺ
    34Ôćĺ**Context:** The setup-forge workflow detects available tools and assigns a tier.
    35Ôćĺ
    36Ôćĺ**Implementation:** Each tool is verified by executing its version command ÔÇö presence in PATH is insufficient. The tier is the highest level where all required tools pass verification:
    37Ôćĺ
    38Ôćĺ```
    39Ôćĺast-grep --version  Ôćĺ required for Forge and above
    40Ôćĺccc --help + ccc doctor Ôćĺ required for Forge+
    41Ôćĺgh --version        Ôćĺ required for Deep
    42Ôćĺqmd status          Ôćĺ required for Deep
    43Ôćĺ```
    44Ôćĺ
    45ÔćĺIf ast-grep passes but gh fails, the tier is Forge (not a "degraded Deep"). If ast-grep and ccc pass but gh and qmd fail, the tier is Forge+ (not a "degraded Deep").
    46Ôćĺ
    47Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
    48Ôćĺ- Tools must be functional, not just installed
    49Ôćĺ- Tier assignment is deterministic ÔÇö no ambiguity
    50Ôćĺ- The result is written to `forge-tier.yaml` in the sidecar and persists across sessions
    51Ôćĺ
    52Ôćĺ### Example 2: Positive Tier Framing
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ**Context:** Reporting the detected tier to the user.
    55Ôćĺ
    56Ôćĺ**Implementation:**
    57Ôćĺ```
    58ÔćĺQuick:  "Fast, template-driven skill creation ÔÇö package-name resolution and
    59Ôćĺ         source reading. Skills ready in minutes."
    60Ôćĺ
    61ÔćĺForge:  "AST-backed structural analysis ÔÇö line-level citations with verified
    62Ôćĺ         signatures. Precision-focused compilation."
    63Ôćĺ
    64ÔćĺForge+: "Semantic-guided precision compilation ÔÇö cocoindex-code maps the codebase
    65Ôćĺ         semantically before AST extraction runs. Every skill begins with a ranked
    66Ôćĺ         discovery pass, then AST-backed verification."
    67Ôćĺ
    68ÔćĺDeep:   "Full intelligence pipeline ÔÇö AST verification plus GitHub exploration
    69Ôćĺ         and QMD knowledge synthesis. Maximum provenance depth."
    70Ôćĺ```
    71Ôćĺ
    72Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
    73Ôćĺ- Each description leads with what the tier does, not what it misses
    74Ôćĺ- No tier mentions another tier's capabilities as something it lacks
    75Ôćĺ- Quick is "fast" not "limited"; Forge is "precise" not "partial"
    76Ôćĺ
    77Ôćĺ### Example 3: Tier-Adaptive Extraction
    78Ôćĺ
    79Ôćĺ**Context:** The create-skill extraction step adapts its strategy based on the forge tier.
    80Ôćĺ
    81Ôćĺ**Implementation:**
    82Ôćĺ- **Quick:** Reads source files, applies pattern matching for exports and signatures, produces T1-low citations. Uses `gh_bridge` for file structure when available as a convenience, not a requirement.
    83Ôćĺ- **Forge:** Uses `ast_bridge.scan_definitions()` for structural parsing, produces T1 citations with exact AST node types. Falls back to source reading for files ast-grep cannot parse.
    84Ôćĺ- **Forge+:** Identical extraction to Forge, plus ccc semantic pre-discovery narrows the file set before ast-grep runs. For large codebases, `ccc_bridge.search()` produces a ranked candidate list. ast-grep operates on that list rather than the full tree. All results remain T1 (AST-verified) ÔÇö ccc is upstream, not in the extraction chain.
    85Ôćĺ- **Deep:** Identical extraction to Forge, plus QMD enrichment in a separate step. T2 annotations layer on top of the T1 base without replacing it.
    86Ôćĺ
    87Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
    88Ôćĺ- Extraction quality increases with tier, but every tier produces complete output
    89Ôćĺ- Forge and Deep share the same extraction core ÔÇö Deep adds enrichment, not better parsing
    90Ôćĺ- Fallback within a tier (e.g., ast-grep fails on one file) degrades gracefully to T1-low for that file only
    91Ôćĺ
    92Ôćĺ### Example 4: Tier-Adaptive Scoring
    93Ôćĺ
    94Ôćĺ**Context:** The test-skill workflow adjusts scoring weights based on the forge tier.
    95Ôćĺ
    96Ôćĺ**Implementation:**
    97Ôćĺ- **Forge/Deep (Naive mode ÔÇö individual skill):** Coherence weight (18%) redistributed to remaining categories: Export Coverage 45%, Signature Accuracy 25%, Type Coverage 20%, External Validation 10%. All four active categories are scored with AST-backed data.
    98Ôćĺ- **Quick (any mode):** Signature Accuracy and Type Coverage are skipped (no AST available). Their weights redistribute proportionally to remaining active categories. For a Quick-tier individual skill (naive mode): after both adjustments (coherence removal + AST-dependent category removal), only Export Coverage and External Validation remain active ÔÇö approximate redistributed weights: Export Coverage ~82%, External Validation ~18% (exact values depend on proportional redistribution; see scoring-rules.md for the calculation). When external validation tools (skill-check, tessl) are also unavailable, their weight is further redistributed, leaving Export Coverage as the sole scoring axis.
    99Ôćĺ- **Forge (Contextual mode):** Export Coverage 36%, Signature Accuracy 22%, Type Coverage 14%, Coherence 18%, External Validation 10%. Full AST-backed verification. When external validation tools are unavailable, their 10% is redistributed proportionally.
   100Ôćĺ- **Forge+ (Contextual mode):** Same weights as Forge. The improved extraction coverage (from ccc pre-ranking) may increase T1 count and reduce gaps, but the scoring weights themselves do not change.
   101Ôćĺ- **Deep (Contextual mode):** Same weights as Forge, plus cross-repo verification and QMD coherence checks feed into the coherence score.
   102Ôćĺ
   103Ôćĺ**Key Points:**
   104Ôćĺ- Pass threshold (80%) is the same across all tiers ÔÇö quality bar is tier-independent
   105Ôćĺ- Weight redistribution reflects what each tier can actually measure
   106Ôćĺ- A Quick skill scoring 85% and a Deep skill scoring 85% both pass ÔÇö different measurements, same bar
   107Ôćĺ
   108Ôćĺ## Graceful Degradation
   109Ôćĺ
   110ÔćĺWhen a tool becomes unavailable mid-session (e.g., ast-grep uninstalled between runs):
   111Ôćĺ1. Setup-forge re-detects and downgrades the tier
   112Ôćĺ2. Existing skills retain their original citations ÔÇö they don't retroactively lose confidence
   113Ôćĺ3. New extractions use the current tier's capabilities
   114Ôćĺ4. The tier change is logged in the sidecar for audit trail
   115Ôćĺ
   116ÔćĺWhen source is remote (GitHub URL) and tier is Forge, Forge+, or Deep:
   117Ôćĺ1. AST extraction requires local files ÔÇö ast-grep cannot operate on remote URLs
   118Ôćĺ2. The extraction step attempts an **ephemeral shallow clone** to a system temp path (if `git` is available)
   119Ôćĺ3. If the clone succeeds: AST extraction proceeds on the local clone, then the temp directory is cleaned up
   120Ôćĺ4. If the clone fails (or `git` is unavailable): the step warns the user explicitly and degrades to source reading (T1-low) with actionable guidance
   121Ôćĺ5. Silent degradation is forbidden ÔÇö the user must always know when AST extraction was skipped and why
   122Ôćĺ
   123Ôćĺ## Anti-Patterns
   124Ôćĺ
   125Ôćĺ- Describing Quick tier as "basic" or "limited" ÔÇö it is fast and legitimate
   126Ôćĺ- Requiring ast-grep for workflows that work fine without it (setup-forge, brief-skill)
   127Ôćĺ- Mixing tier capabilities in a single extraction (e.g., T1 citations when only source reading was used)
   128Ôćĺ- Failing a workflow because the tier is "too low" ÔÇö adapt behavior instead
   129Ôćĺ- Silent degradation when source is remote at Forge/Deep tier ÔÇö always warn with actionable guidance
   130Ôćĺ
   131Ôćĺ## Related Fragments
   132Ôćĺ
   133Ôćĺ- [confidence-tiers.md](confidence-tiers.md) ÔÇö how tier determines available confidence levels
   134Ôćĺ- [skill-lifecycle.md](skill-lifecycle.md) ÔÇö how tier affects workflow selection and artifact flow
   135Ôćĺ- [zero-hallucination.md](zero-hallucination.md) ÔÇö Quick tier has lower confidence but the same integrity standard
   136Ôćĺ
   137Ôćĺ_Source: distilled from forge-tier.yaml, tier-rules.md, and setup-forge workflow_
   138Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# Architecture Verification
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Principle
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺA tech stack is only as strong as its weakest integration point. Architecture verification cross-references generated skills against architecture documents to produce evidence-backed feasibility verdicts ÔÇö before a single line of application code is written. Every verdict must cite specific APIs, types, or function signatures from the generated skills. Speculation is labeled; evidence is cited.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Rationale
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺArchitects commonly evaluate tech stacks by reading documentation, building proof-of-concepts, or asking on forums. This process is slow, incomplete, and leaves integration risks undiscovered until implementation. SKF's pre-code verification pipeline replaces guesswork with systematic cross-referencing:
    10Ôćĺ
    11Ôćĺ1. Generate skills for each candidate library (CS/QS) ÔÇö producing T1/T2 extraction data
    12Ôćĺ2. Run Verify Stack (VS) ÔÇö cross-reference extracted APIs against architecture integration claims
    13Ôćĺ3. Run Refine Architecture (RA) ÔÇö improve the architecture doc with verified API evidence
    14Ôćĺ4. Run Stack Skill compose mode (SS) ÔÇö synthesize a consolidated integration playbook
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺWithout architecture verification:
    17Ôćĺ- Integration risks surface during implementation (costly, late)
    18Ôćĺ- Architecture documents describe intent without evidence
    19Ôćĺ- Library selection is based on feature lists, not API compatibility
    20Ôćĺ
    21ÔćĺWith architecture verification:
    22Ôćĺ- Integration feasibility is validated pre-code with API-level evidence
    23Ôćĺ- Architecture documents become evidence-backed engineering artifacts
    24Ôćĺ- Library swaps happen in minutes (regenerate skill, re-run VS) not days
    25Ôćĺ
    26Ôćĺ## Verification Passes
    27Ôćĺ
    28Ôćĺ### Pass 1: Coverage Verification
    29Ôćĺ
    30ÔćĺCheck that a generated skill exists for every technology referenced in the architecture document. Coverage gaps mean unverified assumptions about library capabilities.
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ- **Covered**: Skill exists ÔÇö APIs are known
    33Ôćĺ- **Missing**: Referenced but no skill ÔÇö capabilities are assumed, not verified
    34Ôćĺ
    35Ôćĺ### Pass 2: Integration Verification
    36Ôćĺ
    37ÔćĺFor each integration claim in the architecture document, cross-reference the API surfaces of both libraries:
    38Ôćĺ
    39Ôćĺ- **Verified**: APIs demonstrably connect ÔÇö matching types, documented bridge, or shared protocol
    40Ôćĺ- **Plausible**: Compatible types or protocols but no documented integration path
    41Ôćĺ- **Risky**: Type mismatch, protocol gap, or language boundary requiring a bridge
    42Ôćĺ- **Blocked**: Fundamental incompatibility ÔÇö no feasible integration path
    43Ôćĺ
    44ÔćĺCross-reference protocol:
    45Ôćĺ1. Language boundary check (same language Ôćĺ direct calls; TypeScriptÔćöRust Ôćĺ needs FFI/IPC)
    46Ôćĺ2. Protocol compatibility (in-process, HTTP, WebSocket, shared filesystem)
    47Ôćĺ3. Type compatibility (does A export something B accepts?)
    48Ôćĺ4. Documentation cross-reference (does either library mention the other?)
    49Ôćĺ
    50Ôćĺ### Pass 3: Requirements Verification
    51Ôćĺ
    52ÔćĺIf a PRD/vision document is provided, verify the stack covers stated capability requirements:
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ- **Fulfilled**: Stack capabilities demonstrably address the requirement
    55Ôćĺ- **Partially Fulfilled**: Some aspects covered, gaps remain
    56Ôćĺ- **Not Addressed**: No library in the stack covers this requirement
    57Ôćĺ
    58Ôćĺ## Iteration Loop
    59Ôćĺ
    60ÔćĺThe core value of architecture verification is the iteration loop:
    61Ôćĺ
    62Ôćĺ1. VS produces verdicts Ôćĺ user reads RISKY/BLOCKED items
    63Ôćĺ2. User swaps a library Ôćĺ generates new skill via CS/QS
    64Ôćĺ3. User re-runs VS Ôćĺ sees delta: "Previously RISKY, now VERIFIED"
    65Ôćĺ4. Repeat until FEASIBLE
    66Ôćĺ
    67ÔćĺEach VS run should produce a diff from the previous run when a prior feasibility report exists. This makes the iteration cycle visible and measurable.
    68Ôćĺ
    69Ôćĺ## Evidence Citation Format
    70Ôćĺ
    71ÔćĺAll verdicts cite evidence from generated skills:
    72Ôćĺ
    73Ôćĺ```
    74ÔćĺLibrary A exports: `function_name(params) Ôćĺ return_type` [from skill: skill-name]
    75ÔćĺLibrary B accepts: `function_name(params)` [from skill: skill-name]
    76ÔćĺCompatibility: {explanation}
    77Ôćĺ```
    78Ôćĺ
    79Ôćĺ## Lifecycle Position
    80Ôćĺ
    81ÔćĺArchitecture verification sits between individual skill generation and stack skill synthesis:
    82Ôćĺ
    83Ôćĺ```
    84Ôćĺ(CS|QS)├ŚN Ôćĺ VS Ôćĺ RA Ôćĺ SS (compose) Ôćĺ TS Ôćĺ EX Ôćĺ Implementation Ôćĺ SS (code-mode)
    85Ôćĺ```
    86Ôćĺ
    87ÔćĺVS and RA are pre-code workflows. SS compose-mode synthesizes the implementation playbook. After the codebase grows, SS code-mode (the standard stack-skill mode that analyzes manifests and source) captures actual usage patterns ÔÇö completing the lifecycle.
    88Ôćĺ
    89Ôćĺ## Anti-Patterns
    90Ôćĺ
    91Ôćĺ- Treating VS verdicts as absolute ÔÇö they are evidence-based assessments, not guarantees
    92Ôćĺ- Running VS without generating skills first ÔÇö VS reads skills, it does not create them
    93Ôćĺ- Skipping the iteration loop ÔÇö the first VS run often reveals gaps that require library swaps
    94Ôćĺ- Using VS output as a SKILL.md ÔÇö VS produces a feasibility report, not a skill; use SS compose-mode for the stack skill
    95Ôćĺ
    96Ôćĺ## Related Fragments
    97Ôćĺ
    98Ôćĺ- [confidence-tiers.md](confidence-tiers.md) ÔÇö T1/T2/T3 trust model for verdict evidence
    99Ôćĺ- [skill-lifecycle.md](skill-lifecycle.md) ÔÇö end-to-end pipeline and workflow connections
   100Ôćĺ- [progressive-capability.md](progressive-capability.md) ÔÇö tier philosophy for skill generation
   101Ôćĺ
   102Ôćĺ_Source: synthesized from verify-stack/workflow.md, refine-architecture/workflow.md, and create-stack-skill compose-mode-rules.md_
   103Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# Split-Body Strategy
     2Ôćĺ
     3Ôćĺ## Principle
     4Ôćĺ
     5ÔćĺSelective split is the recommended strategy when `split-body` is needed. Extract only the largest Tier 2 section(s) to stay under the 500-line body guideline ÔÇö keep all actionable Tier 1 content inline. Full split-body reduces agent accuracy because on-demand retrieval underperforms inline passive context.
     6Ôćĺ
     7Ôćĺ## Rationale
     8Ôćĺ
     9ÔćĺVercel research shows inline passive context achieves 100% task accuracy vs 79% for on-demand retrieval. Full split-body moves all Tier 2 content to `references/`, forcing agents to retrieve it on demand. Selective split preserves the most-used content inline while still satisfying the body size constraint.
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺWithout selective split awareness:
    12Ôćĺ- `skill-check split-body --write` extracts everything, reducing agent effectiveness
    13Ôćĺ- tessl content scores drop dramatically (65% Ôćĺ 38%) because only SKILL.md body is evaluated
    14Ôćĺ- Context snippet anchors (`#quick-start`, `#key-types`) may break if those sections move to references
    15Ôćĺ
    16ÔćĺWith selective split awareness:
    17Ôćĺ- Only the largest Tier 2 section(s) are extracted (usually Full API Reference or Full Type Definitions)
    18Ôćĺ- Quick Start, Key API Summary, Key Types, Migration Warnings stay inline
    19Ôćĺ- Agent accuracy remains high for common tasks
    20Ôćĺ- tessl scores reflect actual inline content quality
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ## Split-Body Detection Pattern
    23Ôćĺ
    24ÔćĺA split-body skill is identified by:
    25Ôćĺ1. A `references/` directory exists alongside SKILL.md
    26Ôćĺ2. SKILL.md Tier 2 sections (headings starting with `## Full`) contain only stubs or are absent ÔÇö their content lives in `references/*.md`
    27Ôćĺ3. The skill's full documented content spans SKILL.md + `references/*.md`
    28Ôćĺ
    29ÔćĺWhen processing a split-body skill, any workflow step that reads SKILL.md content must also traverse `references/*.md` to get the complete picture.
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ## Anti-Patterns
    32Ôćĺ
    33Ôćĺ- Running `skill-check split-body --write` without first attempting selective split
    34Ôćĺ- Moving Tier 1 sections (Quick Start, Key API Summary) to references/ ÔÇö these must stay inline
    35Ôćĺ- Splitting before checking whether context snippet anchors will break
    36Ôćĺ
    37Ôćĺ## Recommended Approach
    38Ôćĺ
    39ÔćĺWhen the 500-line body guideline is exceeded:
    40Ôćĺ1. Identify which Tier 2 section(s) are largest (usually `## Full API Reference`)
    41Ôćĺ2. Extract only those specific sections to `references/`
    42Ôćĺ3. Keep all Tier 1 sections and smaller Tier 2 sections inline
    43Ôćĺ4. Verify context snippet anchors still resolve after extraction
    44Ôćĺ
    45Ôćĺ## Scripts and Assets Interaction
    46Ôćĺ
    47ÔćĺSplit-body affects only SKILL.md content movement to `references/`. The `scripts/` and `assets/` directories are unaffected ÔÇö they remain as top-level siblings of SKILL.md regardless of split-body decisions. They do not count toward the body line limit.
    48Ôćĺ
    49Ôćĺ## Related Fragments
    50Ôćĺ
    51Ôćĺ- [agentskills-spec.md](agentskills-spec.md) ÔÇö 500-line guideline for SKILL.md body size and the `references/` directory structure
    52Ôćĺ- [skill-lifecycle.md](skill-lifecycle.md) ÔÇö where split-body decisions fit in the compilation pipeline
    53Ôćĺ- `test-skill/data/scoring-rules.md` ÔÇö tessl/split-body interaction and the pre-split baseline recommendation during test reporting
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ_Source: derived from agentskills.io split-body guidance and Vercel agent accuracy research (inline vs on-demand retrieval)_
    56Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ---
     2Ôćĺtitle: Examples
     3Ôćĺdescription: Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting for Skill Forge
     4Ôćĺ---
     5Ôćĺ
     6Ôćĺ# Examples & Use Cases
     7Ôćĺ
     8ÔćĺThis section provides practical examples for using SKF: Skill Forge.
     9Ôćĺ
    10Ôćĺ---
    11Ôćĺ
    12Ôćĺ## What the Output Looks Like
    13Ôćĺ
    14ÔćĺWhen SKF generates a skill, you get a `SKILL.md` file with machine-readable frontmatter and provenance-backed instructions. Here's a trimmed example from a real skill generated for [cognee](https://github.com/topoteretes/cognee) (browse the full output at [oh-my-skills](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills)):
    15Ôćĺ
    16Ôćĺ**Frontmatter (tells AI agents when to load this skill):**
    17Ôćĺ
    18Ôćĺ```yaml
    19Ôćĺname: cognee
    20Ôćĺdescription: Use when cognee is a Python AI memory engine that transforms
    21Ôćĺ  documents into knowledge graphs with vector and graph storage for semantic
    22Ôćĺ  search and reasoning. Use this skill when writing code that calls cognee's
    23Ôćĺ  Python API (add, cognify, search, memify, config, datasets, prune, session).
    24Ôćĺ```
    25Ôćĺ
    26Ôćĺ**Body (what your AI agent reads):**
    27Ôćĺ
    28Ôćĺ```
    29Ôćĺ## Key API Summary
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ| Function | Purpose | Key Params | Source |
    32Ôćĺ|----------|---------|------------|--------|
    33Ôćĺ| add() | Ingest text, files, binary data | data, dataset_name | [AST:cognee/api/v1/add/add.py:L22] |
    34Ôćĺ| cognify() | Build knowledge graph | datasets, graph_model | [AST:cognee/api/v1/cognify/cognify.py:L47] |
    35Ôćĺ| search() | Query knowledge graph | query_text, query_type | [AST:cognee/api/v1/search/search.py:L26] |
    36Ôćĺ| memify() | Enrich graph with custom tasks | extraction_tasks, data | [AST:cognee/modules/memify/memify.py:L27] |
    37Ôćĺ| session.* | Session history and feedback | get_session(), add_feedback() | [SRC:cognee/api/v1/session/__init__.py:L8] |
    38Ôćĺ| DataPoint | Base class for custom graph nodes | inherit and add fields | [EXT:docs.cognee.ai/guides/custom-data-models] |
    39Ôćĺ```
    40Ôćĺ
    41ÔćĺProvenance tags trace each instruction to its source:
    42Ôćĺ- `[AST:file:line]` ÔÇö extracted from code via AST parsing (highest confidence)
    43Ôćĺ- `[SRC:file:line]` ÔÇö read from source code without AST verification
    44Ôćĺ- `[EXT:url]` ÔÇö sourced from external documentation
    45Ôćĺ- `[QMD:collection:doc]` ÔÇö surfaced from indexed developer discourse (issues, PRs, changelogs)
    46Ôćĺ
    47ÔćĺSee [How It Works](../how-it-works/) for the full output structure.
    48Ôćĺ
    49Ôćĺ**Full skill directory structure:**
    50Ôćĺ
    51Ôćĺ```
    52Ôćĺskills/cognee/
    53ÔćĺÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ SKILL.md              # What your agent reads
    54ÔćĺÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ context-snippet.md    # Compressed index for CLAUDE.md
    55ÔćĺÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ metadata.json         # Machine-readable provenance
    56ÔćĺÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ references/           # Progressive disclosure detail
    57ÔćĺÔöé   ÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ api-core.md
    58ÔćĺÔöé   ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ graph-types.md
    59ÔćĺÔöťÔöÇÔöÇ scripts/              # Executable utilities (when detected)
    60ÔćĺÔöé   ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ setup-graphdb.sh
    61ÔćĺÔööÔöÇÔöÇ assets/               # Templates and schemas (when detected)
    62Ôćĺ    ÔööÔöÇÔöÇ config-schema.json
    63Ôćĺ```
    64Ôćĺ
    65ÔćĺThe `scripts/` and `assets/` directories appear only when the source repository contains them. Each file traces back to its source with provenance citations and SHA-256 hashes.
    66Ôćĺ
    67Ôćĺ---
    68Ôćĺ
    69Ôćĺ## Example Workflows
    70Ôćĺ
    71Ôćĺ### Quick Skill ÔÇö 47 Seconds
    72Ôćĺ
    73ÔćĺDeveloper adds [cognee](https://github.com/topoteretes/cognee) to a Python project for AI memory management. Agent keeps hallucinating method signatures and config options.
    74Ôćĺ
    75Ôćĺ```
    76Ôćĺ@Ferris QS https://github.com/topoteretes/cognee
    77Ôćĺ```
    78Ôćĺ
    79ÔćĺFerris reads the repository, extracts the public API via source reading, validates against spec. Skill appears in `skills/cognee/`. Agent stops hallucinating. Forty-seven seconds. Done.
    80Ôćĺ
    81Ôćĺ### Brownfield Platform ÔÇö 8 Minutes
    82Ôćĺ
    83ÔćĺAlex's team adopts BMAD for 10 microservices (TypeScript, Go, Rust).
    84Ôćĺ
    85Ôćĺ```
    86Ôćĺ@Ferris SF          # Setup ÔÇö Deep mode detected
    87Ôćĺ@Ferris AN          # Analyze ÔÇö 10 services mapped
    88Ôćĺ@Ferris CS --batch  # Create ÔÇö batch generation
    89Ôćĺ```
    90Ôćĺ
    91Ôćĺ10 individual skills + 1 platform stack skill. BMM architect navigates cross-service flows with verified knowledge.
    92Ôćĺ
    93Ôćĺ### Release Prep ÔÇö Trust Builder
    94Ôćĺ
    95ÔćĺSarah prepares v3.0.0 with breaking changes.
    96Ôćĺ
    97Ôćĺ```
    98Ôćĺ@Ferris AS    # Audit ÔÇö finds 3 renames, 1 removal, 1 addition
    99Ôćĺ@Ferris US    # Update ÔÇö preserves [MANUAL] sections, adds annotations
   100Ôćĺ@Ferris TS    # Test ÔÇö verify completeness
   101Ôćĺ@Ferris EX    # Export ÔÇö package for npm release
   102Ôćĺ```
   103Ôćĺ
   104ÔćĺShips with npm release. Consumers upgrade ÔÇö their agents use the correct function names. Zero hallucination tickets.
   105Ôćĺ
   106Ôćĺ### Stack Skill ÔÇö Integration Intelligence
   107Ôćĺ
   108ÔćĺArmel's full-stack project: Next.js + Serwist + SpacetimeDB + better-auth.
   109Ôćĺ
   110Ôćĺ```
   111Ôćĺ@Ferris SS
   112Ôćĺ```
   113Ôćĺ
   114ÔćĺFerris detects 8 significant dependencies, finds 5 co-import integration points. Generates a consolidated stack skill. The agent now knows: "When you modify the auth flow, update the Serwist cache exclusion at `src/sw.ts:L23`." Integration intelligence no other tool provides.
   115Ôćĺ
   116Ôćĺ### Pre-Code Architecture Verification ÔÇö Greenfield Confidence
   117Ôćĺ
   118ÔćĺJordan is designing a new TypeScript backend with Hono + Drizzle + SpacetimeDB. Architecture doc written, but no code yet. Wants to verify the stack works before building.
   119Ôćĺ
   120Ôćĺ```
   121Ôćĺ@Ferris QS hono          # Quick Skill per library
   122Ôćĺ@Ferris QS drizzle-orm
   123Ôćĺ@Ferris QS spacetimedb-sdk
   124Ôćĺ@Ferris VS               # Verify Stack ÔÇö feasibility report
   125Ôćĺ@Ferris RA               # Refine Architecture ÔÇö enrich with API evidence
   126Ôćĺ@Ferris SS               # Stack Skill ÔÇö compose-mode (no codebase needed)
   127Ôćĺ```
   128Ôćĺ
   129ÔćĺVS finds a Risky integration between Drizzle and SpacetimeDB (incompatible query models) and returns CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE. Jordan adds a bridge layer to the architecture, re-runs VS Ôćĺ FEASIBLE. RA fills in verified API signatures. SS compose-mode synthesizes the stack skill from existing skills + refined architecture. The agent now has integration intelligence for a project that doesn't have code yet.
   130Ôćĺ
   131Ôćĺ---
   132Ôćĺ
   133Ôćĺ## Common Scenarios
   134Ôćĺ
   135Ôćĺ### Scenario A: Greenfield + BMM Integration
   136Ôćĺ
   137ÔćĺBMAD user starts a new project. BMM architect suggests skill generation after retrospective.
   138Ôćĺ
   139Ôćĺ```
   140Ôćĺ@Ferris BS    # Brief ÔÇö scope the skill
   141Ôćĺ@Ferris CS    # Create ÔÇö compile from brief
   142Ôćĺ@Ferris TS    # Test ÔÇö verify completeness
   143Ôćĺ@Ferris EX    # Export ÔÇö inject into CLAUDE.md
   144Ôćĺ```
   145Ôćĺ
   146ÔćĺSkills accumulate over sprints. Agent gets smarter every iteration.
   147Ôćĺ
   148Ôćĺ### Scenario B: Multi-Repo Platform
   149Ôćĺ
   150ÔćĺAlex needs cross-service knowledge for 10 microservices.
   151Ôćĺ
   152ÔćĺOne forge project, multiple QMD collections, hub-and-spoke skills with integration patterns.
   153Ôćĺ
   154Ôćĺ### Scenario C: External Dependency
   155Ôćĺ
   156ÔćĺDeveloper needs skills for a library that doesn't have official skills.
   157Ôćĺ
   158Ôćĺ```
   159Ôćĺ@Ferris QS better-auth
   160Ôćĺ```
   161Ôćĺ
   162ÔćĺChecks ecosystem first. If no official skill exists: generates from source. `source_authority: community`.
   163Ôćĺ
   164Ôćĺ### Scenario D: Docs-Only (SaaS/Closed Source)
   165Ôćĺ
   166ÔćĺNo source code available ÔÇö only documentation.
   167Ôćĺ
   168Ôćĺ```
   169Ôćĺ@Ferris BS
   170Ôćĺ# When asked for target, provide documentation URLs:
   171Ôćĺ# https://docs.cognee.ai/
   172Ôćĺ# Ferris sets source_type: "docs-only" and collects doc_urls
   173Ôćĺ@Ferris CS
   174Ôćĺ# step-03 skips (no source), step-03c fetches docs via doc_fetcher
   175Ôćĺ# All content is T3 [EXT:url] confidence. source_authority: community
   176Ôćĺ```
   177Ôćĺ
   178ÔćĺThe brief's `doc_urls` field drives the doc_fetcher step. The agent uses whatever web fetching tool is available in its environment (Firecrawl, WebFetch, curl, etc.) to retrieve documentation as markdown and extract API information with T3 citations.
   179Ôćĺ
   180Ôćĺ---
   181Ôćĺ
   182Ôćĺ## Tips & Tricks
   183Ôćĺ
   184Ôćĺ### Progressive Capability
   185Ôćĺ
   186ÔćĺStart with Quick mode (no setup required), upgrade to Forge (install ast-grep), then Forge+ (install cocoindex-code for semantic discovery), then Deep (install QMD). Each tier builds on the previous ÔÇö you never lose capability.
   187Ôćĺ
   188Ôćĺ### Batch Operations
   189Ôćĺ
   190ÔćĺUse `--batch` with `create-skill` to process multiple briefs at once. Progress is checkpointed ÔÇö if interrupted, re-run `@Ferris CS --batch` and Ferris will resume automatically from where it left off.
   191Ôćĺ
   192Ôćĺ### Stack Skills + Individual Skills
   193Ôćĺ
   194ÔćĺStack skills focus on integration patterns. Individual skills focus on API surface. Use both together for maximum coverage.
   195Ôćĺ
   196Ôćĺ### The Loop
   197Ôćĺ
   198ÔćĺAfter each sprint's refactor, run `@Ferris US` to regenerate changed components. Export updates CLAUDE.md automatically. Skill generation becomes routine ÔÇö like running tests.
   199Ôćĺ
   200Ôćĺ### Best Practices Built In
   201Ôćĺ
   202ÔćĺGenerated skills automatically follow authoring best practices: third-person descriptions for reliable agent discovery, consistent terminology, degrees-of-freedom matching (prescriptive for fragile operations, flexible for creative tasks), and table-of-contents headers in large reference files. Discovery testing recommendations are included in test reports.
   203Ôćĺ
   204Ôćĺ### Scripts & Assets
   205Ôćĺ
   206ÔćĺIf your source repo includes executable scripts (`scripts/`, `bin/`) or static assets (`templates/`, `schemas/`), SKF detects and packages them automatically with provenance tracking. Custom scripts you add to `scripts/[MANUAL]/` are preserved during updates ÔÇö just like `<!-- [MANUAL] -->` markers in SKILL.md.
   207Ôćĺ
   208Ôćĺ---
   209Ôćĺ
   210Ôćĺ## Troubleshooting
   211Ôćĺ
   212Ôćĺ### Common Issues
   213Ôćĺ
   214Ôćĺ**Forge reports ast-grep is unavailable**
   215ÔćĺIf setup-forge reports that ast-grep was not detected, install it to unlock Forge mode: <https://ast-grep.github.io>
   216Ôćĺ
   217Ôćĺ**"No brief found"**
   218ÔćĺRun `@Ferris BS` first to create a skill brief, or use `@Ferris QS` for brief-less generation.
   219Ôćĺ
   220Ôćĺ**"Ecosystem check: official skill exists"**
   221ÔćĺAn official skill already exists for this package. Consider installing it with `npx skills add` instead of generating your own.
   222Ôćĺ
   223Ôćĺ**Quick mode skills have lower confidence**
   224ÔćĺQuick mode reads source without AST analysis. Install ast-grep to upgrade to Forge mode for structural truth (T1 confidence).
   225Ôćĺ
   226Ôćĺ**Want semantic discovery for large codebases?**
   227ÔćĺInstall [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) to unlock Forge+ mode. CCC indexes your codebase and pre-ranks files by semantic relevance before AST extraction, improving coverage on projects with 500+ files.
   228Ôćĺ
   229Ôćĺ---

content.text:
Perform an ultra-deep cross-cutting review of the entire /home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/ project focusing on SYSTEM-WIDE consistency issues that span multiple directories.

Focus areas:

1. **Schema field propagation**: The system defines skill brief schemas in multiple places (src/workflows/analyze-source/data/skill-brief-schema.md, src/workflows/brief-skill/data/skill-brief-schema.md). Compare ALL schema definitions - they should be consistent or have documented reasons for differences.

2. **metadata.json structure**: The metadata.json format is referenced across many files. Verify that ALL references to metadata.json structure (in templates, examples, documentation, validation steps) use the exact same field names and structure.

3. **Confidence scoring**: The system has a confidence/scoring system. Trace how confidence scores are defined (in scoring-rules, progressive-capability), how they're calculated (in workflow steps), how they're stored (in metadata.json), and how they're validated (in validation steps). Look for any mismatches in the scoring pipeline.

4. **Export targets**: The system exports to multiple platforms (.claude/commands, .cursor/rules, etc.). Verify that ALL references to export targets are consistent across workflows, docs, and knowledge files.

5. **Tier system**: Trace the tier system (Forge, Deep, Surface, Quick, Forge+) through the entire codebase. Every reference to tiers should use consistent naming and the tier capabilities should be consistently described.

6. **QMD types**: The system uses QMD (Quality Metadata) types. Verify that QMD type definitions in the registry match usage across all workflows and knowledge files.

7. **File naming conventions**: Check that file naming follows consistent patterns across all workflows (step numbering, data file names, etc.).

8. **Workflow handoffs**: When one workflow references or calls another (e.g., analyze-source feeds into create-skill), verify the data contracts match - the output of one workflow should match the expected input of the next.

Read files across ALL directories to perform this cross-cutting analysis. Report findings with HIGH confidence only. For each finding, provide exact file paths, line numbers, what's wrong, and what it should be.

     8Ôćĺ# Audit Skill

    10Ôćĺ**Goal:** Detect drift between an existing skill and its current source code, producing a severity-graded drift report with AST-backed findings and actionable remediation suggestions.

This is a deterministic analysis workflow ÔÇö every finding must trace to actual code with file:line citations. You enforce the zero-hallucination principle: structural truth over semantic guessing. You bring AST analysis expertise and drift detection methodology, while the source code provides the ground truth.
    13Ôćĺ
    14Ôćĺ---
    15Ôćĺ
    16Ôćĺ## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE
    17Ôćĺ
    18ÔćĺThis uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:
    19Ôćĺ
    20Ôćĺ### Core Principles
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is a self-contained instruction file that is a part of an overall workflow that must be followed exactly
    23Ôćĺ- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only the current step file is in memory ÔÇö never load future step files until told to do so
    24Ôćĺ- **Sequential Enforcement**: Sequence within the step files must be completed in order, no skipping or optimization allowed
    25Ôćĺ- **State Tracking**: Document progress in output file frontmatter using `stepsCompleted` array
    26Ôćĺ- **Append-Only Building**: Build the drift report by appending sections as directed to the output file
    27Ôćĺ- **Tier-Aware Execution**: Adapt analysis depth based on detected forge tier (Quick/Forge/Forge+/Deep) with graceful degradation
    28Ôćĺ
    29Ôćĺ### Step Processing Rules
    30Ôćĺ
    31Ôćĺ1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Always read the entire step file before taking any action
    32Ôćĺ2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute all numbered sections in order, never deviate
    33Ôćĺ3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: If a menu is presented, halt and wait for user selection
    34Ôćĺ4. **CHECK CONTINUATION**: If the step has a menu with Continue as an option, only proceed to next step when user selects 'C' (Continue)
    35Ôćĺ5. **SAVE STATE**: Update `stepsCompleted` in frontmatter before loading next step
    36Ôćĺ6. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, load, read entire file, then execute the next step file
    37Ôćĺ
    38Ôćĺ### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)
    39Ôćĺ
    40Ôćĺ- ­čŤĹ **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
    41Ôćĺ- ­čôľ **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
    42Ôćĺ- ­čÜź **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
    43Ôćĺ- ­čĺż **ALWAYS** update frontmatter of output files when writing the final output for a specific step
    44Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
    45Ôćĺ- ÔĆŞ´ŞĆ **ALWAYS** halt at menus and wait for user input
    46Ôćĺ- ­čôő **NEVER** create mental todo lists from future steps
    47Ôćĺ- ÔÜÖ´ŞĆ **TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK**: If any instruction references a subprocess, subagent, or tool you do not have access to, you MUST still achieve the outcome in your main context thread
    48Ôćĺ- Ôťů YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
    49Ôćĺ
    50Ôćĺ---
    51Ôćĺ
    52Ôćĺ## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ### 1. Module Configuration Loading
    55Ôćĺ
    56ÔćĺLoad and read full config from {project-root}/_bmad/skf/config.yaml and resolve:
    57Ôćĺ
    58Ôćĺ- `project_name`, `output_folder`, `user_name`, `communication_language`, `document_output_language`
    59Ôćĺ- `skills_output_folder`, `forge_data_folder`, `sidecar_path`
    60Ôćĺ- Generate and store `timestamp` as the current date-time in `YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` format. This value is fixed for the entire workflow run and must not be regenerated in subsequent steps.
    61Ôćĺ
    62Ôćĺ### 2. First Step Execution
    63Ôćĺ
    64ÔćĺLoad, read the full file and then execute `./steps-c/step-01-init.md` to begin the workflow.
    65Ôćĺ

input.name: review-templates
input.prompt:
Perform an ultra-deep review of ALL template and schema files in /home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/*/data/ directories.

Focus specifically on:

1. **YAML/JSON syntax in markdown**: Many files embed YAML or JSON examples within markdown. Verify ALL embedded YAML and JSON is syntactically valid. Check for:
   - Missing colons, commas, or brackets
   - Incorrect indentation in YAML
   - Duplicate keys in JSON objects
   - Mismatched quotes
   - Invalid field values

2. **Template variable consistency**: Templates use {{variables}}. Verify that:
   - All template variables are documented
   - Variables used in templates match what the compilation steps provide
   - No undefined variables exist
   - Variable naming is consistent (snake_case, etc.)

3. **Schema-template alignment**: Where a schema defines expected fields and a template provides a skeleton, verify they match exactly. The template should have ALL required schema fields and no fields that aren't in the schema.

4. **Validation rules**: Where validation rules reference specific fields or values, verify those fields/values exist in the corresponding schemas and templates.

5. **Default values**: Check that default values mentioned in schemas match defaults in templates and defaults in documentation.

6. **Enum values**: Where fields have enumerated allowed values, verify the enum lists are consistent everywhere they appear.

7. **Required vs optional fields**: Verify that required/optional field designations are consistent between schemas, templates, and validation steps.

Read ALL data/ directories across all workflows. Compare schemas, templates, and validation rules against each other. Report findings with HIGH confidence only. For each finding, provide exact file paths, line numbers, what's wrong, and what it should be.

     8Ôćĺ
     9Ôćĺ# Step 1: Load Brief
    10Ôćĺ
    11Ôćĺ## STEP GOAL:
    12Ôćĺ
    13ÔćĺTo load and validate the skill-brief.yaml compilation config, resolve the source code location, and load the forge tier from sidecar to determine available capabilities for the compilation pipeline.
    14Ôćĺ
    15Ôćĺ## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):
    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺ### Universal Rules:
    18Ôćĺ
    19Ôćĺ- ­čôľ CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
    20Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» ALWAYS follow the exact instructions in the step file
    21Ôćĺ- ÔÜÖ´ŞĆ TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK: If any instruction references a tool you do not have access to, you MUST still achieve the outcome in your main context thread
    22Ôćĺ- Ôťů YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
    23Ôćĺ
    24Ôćĺ### Role Reinforcement:
    25Ôćĺ
    26Ôćĺ- Ôťů You are a skill compilation engine performing initialization
    27Ôćĺ- Ôťů This is an autonomous init step ÔÇö no user interaction needed
    28Ôćĺ- Ôťů Load data, validate structure, resolve paths, determine capabilities
    29Ôćĺ
    30Ôćĺ### Step-Specific Rules:
    31Ôćĺ
    32Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» Focus ONLY on loading brief, resolving source, and determining tier
    33Ôćĺ- ­čÜź FORBIDDEN to begin extraction or compilation ÔÇö that's steps 03-05
    34Ôćĺ- ­čÜź FORBIDDEN to write any output files ÔÇö this step only loads and validates
    35Ôćĺ- ­čĺČ Report initialization status clearly
    36Ôćĺ
    37Ôćĺ## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:
    38Ôćĺ
    39Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» Follow MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
    40Ôćĺ- ­čĺż All loaded data remains in context for subsequent steps
    41Ôćĺ- ­čôľ Validate brief structure before proceeding
    42Ôćĺ- ­čÜź Halt with actionable error if prerequisites are missing
    43Ôćĺ
    44Ôćĺ## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:
    45Ôćĺ
    46Ôćĺ- Available: User's skill name or brief path from Ferris menu invocation
    47Ôćĺ- Focus: Loading inputs and determining capabilities
    48Ôćĺ- Limits: Do NOT begin any extraction or compilation work
    49Ôćĺ- Dependencies: forge-tier.yaml must exist (from setup-forge workflow)
    50Ôćĺ
    51Ôćĺ## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
    52Ôćĺ
    53Ôćĺ**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.
    54Ôćĺ
    55Ôćĺ### 1. Load Forge Tier
    56Ôćĺ
    57ÔćĺLoad `{forgeTierFile}` completely.
    58Ôćĺ
    59Ôćĺ**If file does not exist:**
    60ÔćĺHalt with: "Forge halted: No forge configuration found. Run [SF] Setup Forge first to detect tools and set your tier."
    61Ôćĺ
    62Ôćĺ**If file exists:**
    63ÔćĺExtract and report:
    64Ôćĺ- `tier`: Quick, Forge, Forge+, or Deep
    65Ôćĺ- `tools`: which tools are available (gh, ast-grep, ccc, qmd)
    66Ôćĺ- `ccc_index`: ccc index state (status, indexed_path, last_indexed) ÔÇö needed by step-02b
    67Ôćĺ
    68Ôćĺ**Apply tier override:** Read `{preferencesFile}`. If `tier_override` is set and is a valid tier value (Quick, Forge, Forge+, or Deep), use it instead of the detected tier.
    69Ôćĺ
    70Ôćĺ### 2. Discover Skill Brief
    71Ôćĺ
    72Ôćĺ**If user provided a specific brief path or skill name:**
    73Ôćĺ- If the value looks like a file path (starts with `/`, `./`, `~`, or contains path separators): treat it as a direct file path and load it
    74Ôćĺ- Otherwise, treat it as a skill name and search `{forge_data_folder}/{skill-name}/skill-brief.yaml`
    75Ôćĺ- If found, load it completely
    76Ôćĺ
    77Ôćĺ**If user invoked with --batch flag:**
    78Ôćĺ- Check `{sidecar_path}/batch-state.yaml` for an active batch checkpoint:
    79Ôćĺ  - If `batch_active: true` and `current_index` is valid: load the brief at `brief_list[current_index]` (resuming a batch loop from step-08)
    80Ôćĺ  - If no checkpoint exists or `batch_active` is false: search specified directory for all `skill-brief.yaml` files, list discovered briefs with skill names, store list for batch loop processing, and load the FIRST brief
    81Ôćĺ
    82Ôćĺ**If no brief found:**
    83ÔćĺHalt with: "No skill brief found. Run [BS] Brief Skill to create one, or use [QS] Quick Skill for brief-less generation."
    84Ôćĺ
    85Ôćĺ### 3. Validate Brief Structure
    86Ôćĺ
    87ÔćĺCheck that the loaded skill-brief.yaml contains required fields:
    88Ôćĺ
    89Ôćĺ**Required fields:**
    90Ôćĺ- `name` ÔÇö skill identifier (kebab-case)
    91Ôćĺ- `version` ÔÇö source version to compile against
    92Ôćĺ- `source_repo` ÔÇö GitHub owner/repo or local path (**optional when `source_type: "docs-only"`**)
    93Ôćĺ- `language` ÔÇö primary source language
    94Ôćĺ- `scope` ÔÇö what to extract (e.g., "all public exports", specific modules)
    95Ôćĺ
    96Ôćĺ**Optional fields:**
    97Ôćĺ- `source_type` ÔÇö `"source"` (default) or `"docs-only"` (external documentation only)
    98Ôćĺ- `doc_urls` ÔÇö array of `{url, label}` documentation URLs (required when `source_type: "docs-only"`)
    99Ôćĺ- `source_branch` ÔÇö branch to use (default: main/master)
   100Ôćĺ- `source_authority` ÔÇö official/community/internal (default: community; forced to `community` for docs-only)
   101Ôćĺ- `include_patterns` ÔÇö file glob patterns to include
   102Ôćĺ- `exclude_patterns` ÔÇö file glob patterns to exclude
   103Ôćĺ- `description` ÔÇö human description of the skill
   104Ôćĺ- `scripts_intent` ÔÇö `"none"` to skip scripts detection, omit for default auto-detection
   105Ôćĺ- `assets_intent` ÔÇö `"none"` to skip assets detection, omit for default auto-detection
   106Ôćĺ
   107Ôćĺ**Docs-only validation:** When `source_type: "docs-only"`, `source_repo` is not required but `doc_urls` must have at least one entry. `source_authority` is forced to `community`.
   108Ôćĺ
   109Ôćĺ**If required fields missing:**
   110ÔćĺHalt with specific error: "Brief validation failed: missing required field `{field}`. Update your skill-brief.yaml and re-run."
   111Ôćĺ
   112Ôćĺ### 4. Resolve Source Code Location
   113Ôćĺ
   114Ôćĺ**If `source_type: "docs-only"`:** Skip source resolution. Set `source_location: null` in context. Proceed directly to section 5 (Report Initialization) ÔÇö docs-only skills have no source to resolve.
   115Ôćĺ
   116Ôćĺ**If source_repo is a GitHub URL or owner/repo format:**
   117Ôćĺ- Verify repository exists via `gh_bridge.list_tree(owner, repo, branch)`
   118Ôćĺ- If branch not specified, detect default branch
   119Ôćĺ- Store resolved: owner, repo, branch, file tree
   120Ôćĺ
   121Ôćĺ**If source_repo is a local path:**
   122Ôćĺ- Verify path exists and contains source files
   123Ôćĺ- Store resolved: local path, file listing
   124Ôćĺ
   125Ôćĺ**If source cannot be resolved:**
   126ÔćĺHalt with: "Source not found: `{source_repo}`. Verify the repository exists and is accessible."
   127Ôćĺ
   128Ôćĺ### 5. Report Initialization
   129Ôćĺ
   130ÔćĺDisplay initialization summary:
   131Ôćĺ
   132Ôćĺ"**Forge initialized.**
   133Ôćĺ
   134Ôćĺ**Skill:** {name} v{version}
   135Ôćĺ**Source:** {source_repo} @ {branch}
   136Ôćĺ**Language:** {language}
   137Ôćĺ**Scope:** {scope}
   138Ôćĺ**Tier:** {tier} ÔÇö {tier_description}
   139Ôćĺ**Tools:** {available_tools_list}
   140Ôćĺ
   141ÔćĺProceeding to ecosystem check..."
   142Ôćĺ
   143ÔćĺWhere tier_description follows positive capability framing:
   144Ôćĺ- Quick: "Source reading and spec validation"
   145Ôćĺ- Forge: "AST-backed structural extraction"
   146Ôćĺ- Forge+: "Semantic-guided precision ÔÇö ccc pre-ranks files before AST extraction"
   147Ôćĺ- Deep: "Full intelligence ÔÇö structural + contextual + QMD knowledge synthesis"
   148Ôćĺ
   149Ôćĺ### 6. Menu Handling Logic
   150Ôćĺ
   151Ôćĺ**Auto-proceed step ÔÇö no user interaction.**
   152Ôćĺ
   153ÔćĺAfter initialization is complete and all data is loaded, immediately load, read entire file, then execute `{nextStepFile}`.
   154Ôćĺ
   155Ôćĺ#### EXECUTION RULES:
   156Ôćĺ
   157Ôćĺ- This is an auto-proceed initialization step with no user choices
   158Ôćĺ- Proceed directly to next step after successful initialization
   159Ôćĺ- If any prerequisite check fails, HALT with actionable error ÔÇö do NOT proceed
   160Ôćĺ
   161Ôćĺ## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE
   162Ôćĺ
   163ÔćĺONLY WHEN forge-tier.yaml is loaded, skill-brief.yaml is validated, and source code location is resolved will you proceed to load `{nextStepFile}` for ecosystem check.
   164Ôćĺ
   165Ôćĺ---
   166Ôćĺ
   167Ôćĺ## ­čÜĘ SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS
   168Ôćĺ
   169Ôćĺ### Ôťů SUCCESS:
   170Ôćĺ
   171Ôćĺ- Forge tier loaded from sidecar with tool availability
   172Ôćĺ- Skill brief loaded and all required fields validated
   173Ôćĺ- Source code location resolved and accessible (or `source_location: null` confirmed for docs-only skills)
   174Ôćĺ- Initialization summary displayed with tier and capabilities
   175Ôćĺ- Auto-proceeded to step-02
   176Ôćĺ
   177Ôćĺ### ÔŁî SYSTEM FAILURE:
   178Ôćĺ
   179Ôćĺ- Proceeding without forge-tier.yaml (missing prerequisite)
   180Ôćĺ- Proceeding with invalid or incomplete brief
   181Ôćĺ- Not resolving source code location before proceeding
   182Ôćĺ- Beginning extraction or compilation work in this step
   183Ôćĺ- Not displaying initialization summary
   184Ôćĺ
   185Ôćĺ**Master Rule:** This step ONLY loads and validates. It does NOT extract, compile, or write files.
   186Ôćĺ

     7Ôćĺ# Step 2: Ecosystem Check
     8Ôćĺ
     9Ôćĺ## STEP GOAL:
    10Ôćĺ
    11ÔćĺTo search the agentskills.io ecosystem for an existing official skill matching the brief, advise the user if one is found, and allow them to decide whether to proceed with compilation or install the existing skill.
    12Ôćĺ
    13Ôćĺ## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):
    14Ôćĺ
    15Ôćĺ### Universal Rules:
    16Ôćĺ
    17Ôćĺ- ­čôľ CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
    18Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» ALWAYS follow the exact instructions in the step file
    19Ôćĺ- ÔÜÖ´ŞĆ TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK: If any instruction references a tool you do not have access to, you MUST still achieve the outcome in your main context thread
    20Ôćĺ- Ôťů YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
    21Ôćĺ
    22Ôćĺ### Role Reinforcement:
    23Ôćĺ
    24Ôćĺ- Ôťů You are a skill compilation engine performing ecosystem discovery
    25Ôćĺ- Ôťů This step is advisory ÔÇö ecosystem check results never block compilation
    26Ôćĺ- Ôťů Tool unavailability is never an error ÔÇö it's a graceful skip condition
    27Ôćĺ
    28Ôćĺ### Step-Specific Rules:
    29Ôćĺ
    30Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» Focus ONLY on ecosystem search and presenting findings
    31Ôćĺ- ­čÜź FORBIDDEN to begin any extraction or compilation work
    32Ôćĺ- ­čÜź FORBIDDEN to halt the workflow if the ecosystem check fails or times out
    33Ôćĺ- ­čĺČ If a match is found, present it factually ÔÇö let the user decide
    34Ôćĺ- ÔĆ▒´ŞĆ Enforce 5-second timeout on ecosystem queries
    35Ôćĺ
    36Ôćĺ## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:
    37Ôćĺ
    38Ôćĺ- ­čÄ» Follow MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
    39Ôćĺ- ­čĺż All loaded data remains in context for subsequent steps
    40Ôćĺ- ­čôľ Ecosystem check is advisory ÔÇö never blocking
    41Ôćĺ- ­čÜź Do not modify any files ÔÇö this step only queries and reports
    42Ôćĺ
    43Ôćĺ## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:
    44Ôćĺ
    45Ôćĺ- Available: brief_data (name, version, source_repo, language, scope) and tier from step-01
    46Ôćĺ- Focus: Searching for existing skills in the ecosystem
    47Ôćĺ- Limits: Do NOT begin extraction, compilation, or file writing
    48Ôćĺ- Dependencies: skill-brief.yaml must be loaded and validated from step-01
    49Ôćĺ
    50Ôćĺ## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
    51Ôćĺ
    52Ôćĺ**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.
    53Ôćĺ
    54Ôćĺ### 1. Check Ecosystem for Existing Skill
    55Ôćĺ
    56Ôćĺ**Note:** Ecosystem lookup requires the agentskills.io registry API, which is not yet available. The `skill-check` CLI validates local skills but does not query a remote registry.
    57Ôćĺ
    58Ôćĺ**If agentskills.io registry API is available:**
    59Ôćĺ
    60ÔćĺQuery the ecosystem using the skill name from the brief:
    61Ôćĺ- Call the registry API with brief.name ÔÇö check if an official skill already exists
    62Ôćĺ- Enforce 5-second timeout ÔÇö if the query does not return within 5 seconds, treat as no match
    63Ôćĺ- Cache results for 24 hours (if re-running same skill)
    64Ôćĺ
    65Ôćĺ**If registry API is NOT available (current default):**
    66Ôćĺ
    67ÔćĺSkip completely and silently. Do not output any message about API unavailability or the ecosystem check being skipped. Emit zero text to the console. Proceed exactly as if no match was found.
    68Ôćĺ
    69Ôćĺ### 2. Evaluate Results
    70Ôćĺ
    71Ôćĺ**If match found (official skill exists):**
    72Ôćĺ
    73ÔćĺPresent the finding to the user:
    74Ôćĺ
    75Ôćĺ"**Ecosystem match found.**
    76Ôćĺ
    77Ôćĺ**Existing skill:** {matched_skill_name} v{matched_version}
    78Ôćĺ**Source:** {matched_source}
    79Ôćĺ**Authority:** {official/community}
    80Ôćĺ**Compatibility:** {compatibility_notes}
    81Ôćĺ
    82ÔćĺAn existing skill already covers this source. You can:
    83Ôćĺ- **[P] Proceed** ÔÇö compile your own version anyway (useful for customization or different scope)
    84Ôćĺ- **[I] Install** ÔÇö install the existing skill instead of compiling
    85Ôćĺ- **[A] Abort** ÔÇö cancel this compilation"
    86Ôćĺ
    87Ôćĺ**If no match found:**
    88Ôćĺ
    89ÔćĺAuto-proceed silently to next step. Do not display any message about the ecosystem check ÔÇö absence of a match is the expected case.
    90Ôćĺ
    91Ôćĺ**If timeout or error:**
    92Ôćĺ
    93ÔćĺAuto-proceed silently. Log a note in context: "Ecosystem check skipped (timeout/error) ÔÇö proceeding with compilation."
    94Ôćĺ
    95Ôćĺ### 3. Menu Handling Logic
    96Ôćĺ
    97Ôćĺ**Conditional menu ÔÇö only displayed if a match was found.**
    98Ôćĺ
    99Ôćĺ#### If Match Found ÔÇö Present MENU OPTIONS:
   100Ôćĺ
   101ÔćĺDisplay: "**Ecosystem match found ÔÇö Select an Option:** [P] Proceed with compilation [I] Install existing [A] Abort"
   102Ôćĺ
   103Ôćĺ#### EXECUTION RULES:
   104Ôćĺ
   105Ôćĺ- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
   106Ôćĺ- This menu ONLY appears when an ecosystem match is found
   107Ôćĺ- If no match, timeout, or tool unavailable ÔÇö auto-proceed with no menu
   108Ôćĺ
   109Ôćĺ#### Menu Handling Logic:
   110Ôćĺ
   111Ôćĺ- IF P: Note user's decision to proceed despite existing skill. Immediately load, read entire file, then execute `{nextStepFile}`
   112Ôćĺ- IF I: Display: "Install the existing skill using: `[SF] Setup Forge Ôćĺ install {matched_skill_name}`" then halt workflow. Do not proceed to extraction.
   113Ôćĺ- IF A: Display: "Compilation aborted. Return to Ferris menu to select another action." then halt workflow.
   114Ôćĺ- IF no match/timeout/error: Auto-proceed ÔÇö immediately load, read entire file, then execute `{nextStepFile}`
   115Ôćĺ- IF Any other comments or queries: help user respond then redisplay the menu
   116Ôćĺ
   117Ôćĺ## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE
   118Ôćĺ
   119ÔćĺONLY WHEN the ecosystem check is complete (match evaluated, user decision made if applicable) will you proceed to load `{nextStepFile}` for source extraction.
   120Ôćĺ
   121ÔćĺIf no match is found, this step auto-proceeds with no user interaction.
   122Ôćĺ
   123Ôćĺ---
   124Ôćĺ
   125Ôćĺ## ­čÜĘ SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS
   126Ôćĺ
   127Ôćĺ### Ôťů SUCCESS:
   128Ôćĺ
   129Ôćĺ- Ecosystem check attempted via registry API (or gracefully skipped if API unavailable)
   130Ôćĺ- 5-second timeout enforced on ecosystem queries
   131Ôćĺ- Match presented factually with clear options if found
   132Ôćĺ- No-match case auto-proceeds silently
   133Ôćĺ- User decision respected (Proceed/Install/Abort)
   134Ôćĺ- Auto-proceeded to step-02b (when no match or user chose Proceed)
   135Ôćĺ
   136Ôćĺ### ÔŁî SYSTEM FAILURE:
   137Ôćĺ
   138Ôćĺ- Halting the workflow because ecosystem check failed or timed out
   139Ôćĺ- Treating API unavailability as an error
   140Ôćĺ- Confusing `npx skill-check check` (local validation) with ecosystem lookup
   141Ôćĺ- Displaying "no match found" messages (absence should be silent)
   142Ôćĺ- Beginning extraction or compilation work in this step
   143Ôćĺ- Proceeding without user decision when a match IS found
   144Ôćĺ- Not enforcing the 5-second timeout
   145Ôćĺ
   146Ôćĺ**Master Rule:** This step is advisory. API unavailability and timeouts are silent skips, not errors. Only a confirmed match requires user interaction.
   147Ôćĺ

1Ôćĺ# Ferris Sidecar: Forge Tier State
